So I lead customer operations for GrowthX and have been...
I've for a little over three months now.
We are a pretty young company.
We're about, we're a little over a year old.
We're just doing our first one year renewals now, which is kind of exciting.
But, you know, in terms of my role, I'm thinking about like people, process, and tools that all come together to get our client deliverables out like on time and to spec.
And the technical managing editor role within GrowthX, you know, GrowthX is really a services agency at its heart.
So right now we are delivering growth marketing content to our clients.

And the way that we're doing that is we're leveraging a platform, an internal platform that we've built, that is also about three months old, still pretty new on its own, but it's leveraging, it's leveraging,
Leveraging different AI LLMs to generate workflows that build content for clients.

Some of the ways that we talk about that and make sure that things that we want to make sure that we always have are like a human in the loop throughout the content creation process, even when we're heavily leveraging AI to generate the content.
And then also thinking about kind of the feedback loop and improving the content as well, or improving the product and tool itself as well.
So this technical managing editor role is the core of the pods that make up our delivery org.
So a managing editor has three to five accounts and works with two to three content managers who are the people who are day to day actually using the workflows to generate the content.
So technical.
Well, editor is editing that draft content before it goes to the client.
There's also an aspect of project management in this role.
So you will be, you know, on a weekly basis, making sure that our content that's going out is kind of to the scope of work.
So on average, five pieces of content a week, making sure that's going out, that that's ready to publish.
And then working with the client to kind of keep them abreast of any changes that are happening and kind of our progress against the planned roadmap.

You'll also be working with a account strategist or account director who will be kind of thinking long term high level about, you know, our partnership with the client again, and how we're doing against kind of long term.
From KPIs and goals.
So they'll kind of be laying the vision.
The technical managing editor is making sure that on a week-to-week basis, we're aligned with and meeting those expectations.
And then the content manager is kind of generating that content day-to-day in the platform, our AI workflow platform itself.
There is a technical, the reason that we call this a technical managing editor role is because we do have that product on the back end.
And so part of the editorial job that you'll be doing is thinking about how on an ongoing basis, we can continue to improve the inputs, the prompts that are generating that content itself.
So, you know, you have to have a little bit of that tastemaker spirit to know, okay, this is why the content's good.
This is why the content's bad.
This is how I give that feedback to the engineering team so that they can.
And improve the different steps in the workflow that generate that content.

But I've been working in content marketing for almost 10 years.

And more specifically, when it comes to the work I've done over the last couple of years, my most recent work is I worked as a content marketing manager at a content marketing agency called Animals, which is considered one of the best content marketing agencies in the world for SaaS.
We lots of animals, people.
Yeah, you guys have done a good job of just like bringing in a lot of people.
We agree that they are quite good.
Yeah, and I was there until last fall, no, last spring.
The first two years I was there full time as a content marketing manager, and then the last year was just as a freelancer after they went through a bunch of layoffs.
But what I loved about working at Animals is that we had only four to five clients each as a content marketing manager.
But the processes were very rigorous and very strict in terms of like how we produce content.
I worked with an editor, one of those was Carrie, who we probably know who works there, so she was my editor for two years.
And then we have.
Copy editor, and then sometimes a research team.
So it wasn't just a matter of just like sending in the draft and then getting feedback and then shipping it to the client.

It was very thorough in terms of like, I write an outline, I send it to the editor, they send it back, then I write a 30% outline, they send it back to me, and then we write a 90% draft and then a full draft and then it goes to clients.

So there's a lot of like writing revisions, which at the time kind of like really annoyed me because before I was so used to just shipping content out, but that process at Atmos really taught me how to appreciate high quality content and just having systems and checks and balance and processes and workflows to ensure that high quality content is created.
So it's not just about ranking at the top of search.
It's also just like making sure we create content that meets search requirements, meets the audience's requirements, also meets the brief.
So that's what I really loved about working at Atmos, even though it was just like four to five clients, there was a lot of work.
So I spent a lot of time in the weeds creating.

And then I continued working in content marketing since my time with animals ended last spring, I also right now also work as a content creator, content strategist, content writer, depends on the clients I'm working for, what title they prefer, but I'll say about 70 to 80% of my work is still creating content, what's changed is when I first started at animals, most of that writing was just me and editors.
Now I've really bought AI powered workflows and collaborating with AI in my work, which is what I actually prefer.
I still see a lot of like job posts and people on LinkedIn saying we want someone to write for us but not to use AI, but I prefer creating quality content faster at scale.
So for example, work with an affiliate marketing agency, influence an affiliate marketing agency called impact.com.
100% they allow me to work with AI in my work.
So sometimes I'll do the drafting, and then I'll use AI to, you know, edit, et cetera.
Sometimes I will, sometimes the AI will write the content, and then I'll just go back and check it.
So it's a collaboration between me and AI.
And then another kind of mine is a generative writing tool, generative AI writing tool called Writer.
So basically people use Writer to create content for, you know, their companies, their clients, et cetera.
And what I like about Writer is that I work for Writer.
So I create content for Writer, but I use their own platform to create content for was going to ask you about that.
Which is pretty exciting.
So I love how I managed to just take AI and bring it into my work.
Because before, one of my biggest problems as a writer was sometimes opening the laptop and then sitting with a blank screen and writer's block and all that.
But AI has actually really helped my process in terms of helping me be a better writer, a faster writer.
have, you know, prompts.

Like, AI is taking over my work, what am I going to do?
And then I decided, you know, instead of beating them, let me join them.
So I learned how to use low-code tools and low-code tools to actually build my own workflows, build AI agents.

So I started, like, my own little automation agency, and I help, like, small businesses or people that find me on LinkedIn or Reddit, or I'm part of, like, many, like, no-code, low-code groups online.

Will, talk to me about like your organization style, you know, managing four to five clients, especially with competing priorities.
We talk to clients on a weekly basis.
So how do you think about prioritization and organization?

I think communication and tools like really helps, like the tools, like having Asana, like, you know, to block out, you know, week one, the outline gets sent to the client, week two, it's like, or if it's the same week, now we can do all of that with AI, just like scheduling out like days that the outline gets sent out or the draft gets sent out and feedback.
And then like really like having subtasks within that subtasks within Asana, like really helps.
And then just communicating, because as you know, like when you're working with 10 clients sometimes.
Waiting on a client to send you feedback on something or an outline, and then it doesn't happen, and then that leads to bottlenecks and delays.
So just being able to communicate on, whether it's an Asana or Slack, and being like, hey, client X, I'm still waiting for you to send me this.
If you can't, then the project's going to be behind.
Or letting them know that this is taking much longer than expected.
Can you give us another week?
So just being proactive in terms of communicating within those tools, and then just also using those tools to just organize and, you know, creating timelines.
So the tools and the communication has really helped me in my process.
Yeah, totally.
I want to come back to your AI experience, and like, especially with using Writer, like using the Gen AI tool to create its own content.
Like, what surprised you, or frustrated you in working with AI and content generation?
I think for me, it's just how...
So repetitive and how similar, writer's less because writer has his own like, you know, systems and style guides.

But what I found with other tools, for example, ChatGPT or Cloud, and I'm sure you've seen this yourself, is that when you're on LinkedIn, when you're reading a blog post, can basically tell that something's been written by AI.
Yeah.
Because everything, the words are the same, it's very formulaic.
So I think that's been my greatest frustration in the work that I've seen other people create, is that you can tell it's been created by AI.

But I bring it up because I also tell in my own writing, I could, I'll give a prompt or instruction, and then it will still give, the end dash, or it'll still give like a certain phrase that marks it as AI.
So that's been my frustration.
And the way that I've gotten around that is I talked earlier on about how I have like, editorial writing guidelines.
For example, a client will tell me, oh, I like this piece, but it's, your sentences are too long, or you're repeating certain phrases.
So I keep track of all those things.
And I create.

So now when I write, I feed the AI the specific information, so now it knows not to use certain phrases to switch up the variety the way it speaks, so that was my frustration, but now I found that if you're more specific with, you talked about inputs earlier on and outputs, if you're more careful with the inputs you give it and more specific with the prompts and the instructions, it'll give you the output that you want.

And that's why I mentioned that frustration I see online, is I can clearly tell that someone didn't put in a lot of thought into the inputs they gave the AI, but that was my frustration and I found a way to look around that just for my own experimentation.
Yeah, awesome.

Okay, well, I know that was pretty quick, but you kind of answered some of my questions in your background and, you know, I know you've submitted your writing sample so like those are the main things that we're thinking about or like a experience and, and kind of that project management piece so what question.

Yeah, I'm curious on what, how you would define success for this role, like three months from now, six months a year, because I know, you know, AI is changing and, you know, things, new tools, like new, new ways of thinking, like, how would you constantly be able to say, okay, you're doing great in this role?
Yeah, mean, at a very basic level, thinking about retention, client retention, is a, it's a big reflection on the managing editor, because you're the day to day project manager.

So kind of that flagging, if we're off track, thinking about things like that, that is, I think, the core metric that we are considering right now, in terms of like, high level, or like, the most successful people that we've seen in this role, it's the ability to, like, an analytical mind, which, you know, can be at odds with the editor.

But, you know, with AI and with all of the tools that we have, like, you know, all the analytics tools that we have in content marketing now, being able to connect the dots with what's working, what's not working, and just helping inform that picture of how we're doing against the client's goal is that that's what sets kind of like people who are really good in this role with people who are, excellent in this role.
So I think, like, that's, like, it's not a criteria for failure, but I think the people who excel have that ability to think kind of on two levels.

So one on the editorial side and the second on kind of, like, the data and analytics side, at least just in terms of, like, tracking against metrics and not just thinking about, like, click-through rates, but also thinking about, you know, finding, finding, like,
The indicators and keywords that are where there's an opportunity that are successful and helping think about strategy.
There is like an account strategist role that does support that, that work as well.
But I think the, you know, the more that the managing editor can help do that, the more successful a client will be.
And you just touched on what separates like a good manager or a good managing editor or technical editor from the ones that, you know, don't excel in the role.
But what would you say then, what are the challenges in general?
Like what's some of the challenges that some of the editors encounter, whether it's just like the tools or client expectations or?
Yeah, so I think where people most often get tripped up is leveraging, not leveraging AI enough to get the job.
Um, because part of what we are selling is like content produced at scale.
Um, and so that's essentially can be like 25 pieces of content that you're editing a week.
And so if you're manually editing 25 pieces of content, you're going to be really, really, really stressed out.
Um, and so it's like thinking about, uh, you know, you talked a little bit, we call them artifacts, but all of the like writing guidelines, tone, brand, um, like client persona, all that stuff.
If you have that to inform, um, as you're editing a piece to say, like to ask AI, okay, can you rewrite what's like, this is the first draft that came out of the tool.
Hey, I need you to like take in more into account this persona.
I need you to like reconsider whether you actually followed the writing guide.

Because sometimes it doesn't, you know, it's still, you know, AI is, who knows what it's taking into account at any point in time, but sometimes you're like, these were explicit instructions and you didn't do them.
So, you know, the ability to massage a prompt and continue to go back to the AI and get what you're looking for without having to, like, literally manually edit word by word.
And start to get at least, like, the structure and tone, and then you can edit for language a little bit more.

But, you know, the managing editors, again, who struggle are the ones that, like, they'll do one pass through Claude, and then they'll say, like, I'm going to take this out and I'm going to rewrite it on my own.
And then they're very stressed at the end of the week because they, you know, but we have also good managing editors who can do, who.
You have refined and refined like their cloud projects so that it's between 20 and 40 minutes to edit a piece.
And then that makes it a lot less intense.
And those people are also great because they can then bring the feedback.
Like they can say, this is more with new clients, right?

So like we'll generate a cloud project and then we'll bring kind of the prompts that we used back to the engineering team and say, hey, like now let's refine the pipeline based on like the massaging that we did through cloud.
So it's the ability to like leverage AI to get things done, I think.
And some people struggle with like knowing how to make the most of their time in that way.
Yeah, I can't imagine trying to sit manually through 25 pieces in a week.
It's not possible.
possible.

Without that other side, and so some people are not finding as much success.

I would just say like, that's a growing pain of hiring too fast, and you know, we picked the wrong people for the role just in not, because we need people really fast, and I think the team is scrappy, and they're like willing to work really hard to get things done, and but like, that shouldn't be every week, and we don't want it to be every week, like we don't want people to like work 60 hour weeks or not, I don't think.
